EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent monsoon moisture during the month of August resulted in above normal precipitation for much of eastern Utah. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across the entirety of the zone through the month of August. The extreme (D3) drought improved to the severe (D2) category over the western and central portions of the zone. Meanwhile, the severe drought improved to the moderate (D1) category over the eastern portion of the zone by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent monsoon moisture during the month of August resulted in above normal precipitation for much of eastern Utah. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across portions of the zone through the month of August. The extreme (D3) drought was trimmed back to only include the far southwest border of the zone while severe (D2) conditions persisted elsewhere through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent monsoon moisture during the month of August resulted in above normal precipitation for much of eastern Utah. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged across the area through the month of August. Extreme (D3) drought persisted over the far western corner of the zone with severe (D2) conditions elsewhere through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving shortwave trough acted on residual monsoon moisture to fuel showers and thunderstorms over eastern Utah. Outflow boundaries from decaying thunderstorms resulted in strong winds in Uintah County with a gust of 63 mph, which met high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at McCook Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clouds dissipated in the wake of a cold front which led to strong radiational cooling across eastern Utah. This led to subfreezing temperatures in the valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 28 to 32 degrees across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong and unseasonably cold Pacific trough progressed inland across the southwestern United States. Strong southwest winds occurred out ahead of the trough and along the cold front across all of eastern Utah. Winds gusted to 50 mph or more in the mountains. This led to widespread blowing snow in the higher elevations which, when paired with moderate snow, resulted in significantly reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted in excess of 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air associated with an unseasonably cold Pacific system filtered into the region in its wake. Even with lingering clouds, this led to subfreezing temperatures in the valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows dropped into the 20s across the area, including a low of 21 degrees at the Blanding Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong 160 knot upper level jet associated with an approaching system dug into the Great Basin. As it approached eastern Utah it resulted in a tightening wind gradient with gusty winds at the surface. Winds at the surface gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys. There were localized gusts meeting high wind criteria in the Eastern Uinta Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Fort Duchesne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold airmass in the wake of a cold front resulted in subfreezing temperatures in the Eastern Uinta Basin and Arches/Grand Flat area where vegetation was sensitive to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 30 to 32 degrees across the area, including a low of 30 degrees at the Canyonlands Field Airport in Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong and unseasonably cold Pacific trough progressed inland across the western United States. Strong southwest winds occurred out ahead of the trough and along the cold front across all of eastern Utah. Winds gusted up to 45 mph in the mountains at times. Moderate to heavy snow fell across some of the mountains of eastern Utah for late October with the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 9 to 20 inches of snow fell across the La Sal Mountains with lesser amounts over the Abajos. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant clearing late in the morning resulted in the development of high instability across eastern Utah. This, paired with upper level support and ample moisture, produced strong to severe thunderstorms across the area in the late afternoon and early evening hours. Significant hail accumulation occurred in Green River and along I-70. Additionally, heavy rain occurred with storms which led to flash flooding across portions of Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches to over a foot of dime-sized hail accumulated in Green River and along adjacent portions of I-70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant clearing late in the morning resulted in the development of high instability across eastern Utah. This, paired with upper level support and ample moisture, produced strong to severe thunderstorms across the area in the late afternoon and early evening hours. Significant hail accumulation occurred in Green River and along I-70. Additionally, heavy rain occurred with storms which led to flash flooding across portions of Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms was cleared off I-70 between mile markers 168 and 170 from both the eastbound and westbound lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant clearing late in the morning resulted in the development of high instability across eastern Utah. This, paired with upper level support and ample moisture, produced strong to severe thunderstorms across the area in the late afternoon and early evening hours. Significant hail accumulation occurred in Green River and along I-70. Additionally, heavy rain occurred with storms which led to flash flooding across portions of Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 50 people were stranded overnight in Grand County, Utah after heavy rain from severe thunderstorms wiped out a portion of Mineral Bottom Road which led to a remote boat ramp. The rain completed demolished a section of switchbacks with large boulders accumulating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant clearing late in the morning resulted in the development of high instability across eastern Utah. This, paired with upper level support and ample moisture, produced strong to severe thunderstorms across the area in the late afternoon and early evening hours. Significant hail accumulation occurred in Green River and along I-70. Additionally, heavy rain occurred with storms which led to flash flooding across portions of Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed in Spanish Valley due to flash flooding as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms. This included Shumway Lane and Old City Park, Floy, Hastings, Sunny Acres, and Kerby Roads. Roads remained closed overnight. The Pack Creek gauge on Pack Creek Road reported a rise of 4 feet when about an inch of rain fell in 90 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough slowly moved inland across the western United States. Unsettled westerly flow out of this system, paired with a series of embedded disturbances, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow in the higher elevations. A strong cold front produced heavy snow bands, one of which sat over east-central Utah for several hours. Winds gusted up to 55 mph in the mountains. Bitterly cold temperatures moved into the region in the wake of the trough's passage on New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 18 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with over 2 feet estimated at the La Sal Mountain Upper SNOTEL site. 4 inches fell in the town of Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave disturbances dropped into the Great Basin from the Pacific Northwest. This, paired with above normal precipitable water and jet support aloft, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow for Eastern Utah. Snow bands also resulted in locally higher amounts for some areas. While there was a brief break in activity for some areas between systems on the morning of the 22, some areas saw continuous snowfall through the three-day period. Wind gusts exceeded 30 mph in the valleys throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave disturbances dropped into the Great Basin from the Pacific Northwest. This, paired with above normal precipitable water and jet support aloft, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow for Eastern Utah. Snow bands also resulted in locally higher amounts for some areas. While there was a brief break in activity for some areas between systems on the morning of the 22, some areas saw continuous snowfall through the three-day period. Wind gusts exceeded 30 mph in the valleys throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 33 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy wet snow to the northern Utah mountains, along with strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alta - Collins recorded 3.12 inches of snow water equivalent, with 17 inches of heavy wet snow reported at the site. In addition, Alta Ski Area and Brighton Resort both received 15 inches of snow, with Park City Mountain Resort receiving 14 inches of snow. A wind gust of 95 mph was recorded by the Alta Mt Baldy automated weather station. Other automated weather station wind gusts included 62 mph at Cardiff Peak, 60 mph at Canyons Lookout, and 58 mph at Reynolds Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy wet snow to the northern Utah mountains, along with strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Trial Lake, Chalk Creek, Brown Duck, Lakefork Basin, and Hayden Fork recorded between 1.6 and 1.9 inches of snow water equivalent, or approxmiately 10 to 12 inches of heavy wet snow. In addition, automated weather station recorded wind gusts included 98 mph, 93 mph, and 78 mph at Chepeta, Windy Peak, and Lofty Lake Peak, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy wet snow to the northern Utah mountains, along with strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Farmington, Ben Lomond Peak, Lookout Peak, and Hardscrabble measured 3.8 to 4.1 inches of snow water equivalent, or approximately 20 to 24 inches of heavy wet snow. In addition, automated weather station recorded wind gusts included 64 mph and 60 mph at SR-30 at Laketown Summit and Logan Summit, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough and associated polar jet caused the pressure gradient to tighten over eastern Utah. This, paired with the approach of a surface cold front, resulted in widespread wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. The Eastern Uinta Basin saw gusts up to 58 mph, which met high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by an automated weather station near Vernal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Midlevel instability fueled high based showers across eastern Utah. These virga showers produced gusty outflow winds, one of which resulted in high wind criteria near Vernal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Vernal Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front progressed across eastern Utah and produced scattered to numerous showers. Gusty outflow winds accompanied these showers and there were localized gusts that exceeded 75 mph, which met high wind criteria in the Tavaputs Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 77 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at McCook Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of shortwave troughs lifted out of a broad trough anchored over the Pacific Northwest. The shortwave and associated surface cold front tracked over the Northern Rockies and caused the pressure gradient to tighten aloft. Gusty winds accompanied the system with widespread winds of 45 mph reported. There were localized gusts of greater than 58 mph which met high wind criteria across the Arches and Grand Flat areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Airport in Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system lifted across the region resulting in gusty southwest winds at the surface. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred across eastern Utah. Additionally, virga showers resulted in localized gusts that exceeded 58 mph, which met high wind criteria in the Grand Flat and Arches area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport near Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong and unseasonably cold Pacific trough progressed inland across the southwestern United States. Strong southwest winds occurred out ahead of the trough and along the cold front across all of eastern Utah. Winds gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys. Several areas saw gusts meeting high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by the automated weather station near Monument Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the region. This, paired with the presence of a 120 knot jetstreak aloft, resulted in strong winds at the surface. Widespread wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph occurred across eastern Utah with some areas in east-central Utah reporting gusts up to 58 mph, which met high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport near Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air associated with an unseasonably cold Pacific system filtered into the region in its wake. Even with lingering clouds, this led to subfreezing temperatures in the valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows dropped into the upper teens to mid 20s across the area, including a low of 23 degrees at the Vernal Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clouds continued to dissipate in the wake of an unseasonably strong cold front which led to enhanced radiational cooling across eastern Utah. This led to subfreezing temperatures in the valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows dropped into the 20s across the area, including a low of 25 degrees at the Blanding Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave trough produced breezy conditions across eastern Utah throughout the day, as well as showers during the early evening hours. Given the dry low levels in the valleys, most of these showers were in the form of virga. These virga showers produced even stronger wind gusts with some gusts reaching high wind criteria in the Arches/Grand Flat area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 70 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport near Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several systems, many of them dry, quickly brushed eastern Utah in April which led to precipitation generally trending below normal for the region. This continued below normal precipitation allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged for the majority of eastern Utah. However, areas of extreme drought were confined to southeast Utah with lesser categories elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of April as extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted across the entirety of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several systems moved through eastern Utah in March with precipitation generally trending near to above normal for the northern and central areas. This led to some improvement in the drought conditions across northeast Utah. However, continued below normal precipitation down in southeast Utah allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged across the zone through the month of March. The extreme (D3) drought continued over all but the far eastern portion of the zone where severe (D2) conditions remained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several systems moved through eastern Utah in March with precipitation generally trending near to above normal for the northern and central areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged North American Monsoon pattern prevailed across eastern Utah with abundant moisture present as precipitable water values trended well above normal. Additionally, morning sunshine allowed the atmosphere to rapidly destabilize over the higher terrain. As a result, another round of strong thunderstorms developed across the region. One storm in San Juan County produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted from Monticello looking towards the Abajo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough progressed across eastern Utah. Plenty of upper level support, ample moisture, and instability fueled widespread snow showers across the region with moderate to snow falling in the Eastern Uinta Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open wave Pacific trough lifted across eastern Utah. Ample moisture and orographic support resulted in moderate snow across the Eastern Uintas as well as the La Sal and Abajo Mountains. The heaviest snow fell at elevations above 9000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several systems moved through eastern Utah in March with precipitation generally trending near to above normal for the northern and central areas. This led to some improvement in the drought conditions across northeast Utah. However, continued below normal precipitation down in southeast Utah allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of March as extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted across the entirety of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow and high wind event impacted much of Utah in early November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts includes 1.8 inches of snow water equivalent, or approximately 22 inches of snow, at the Kolob SNOTEL, 1.3 inches of snow water equivalent, or approximately 16 inches of snow, at the Midway Valley SNOTEL, and 1 inch of snow water equivalent, or approximately 12 inches of snow, at the Lonesome Beaver SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern returned in late November, bringing rounds of snow and rain over the final days of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Klondike Narrows, Parley's Summit, and Ben Lomond Peak recorded between 1.0 to 1.3 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 19 to 31 inches of snow. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern returned in late November, bringing rounds of snow and rain over the final days of the month.

Additionally, reports were received of 0.9 inches of snow water equivalent and 19 inches of snow by the BRIGHTON CREST station at Brighton, and 1.0 inches of snow water equivalent and 18 inches of snow by the ALTA - COLLINS station at Alta Ski Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained over the Four Corners region. This led to another round of showers and thunderstorms across southeast Utah. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. A debris flow occurred on the Pack Creek Fire burn scar as a result of a large amount of heavy rain in a short period of time. Record water heights were recorded in Mill Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred on the Pack Creek Fire burn scar as a result of heavy rain. The debris flow in Pack Creek caused flooding over several roadways along the creek. There was also high water and debris in Mill Creek with record water heights recorded. High water moved through Spanish Valley and the outskirts of Moab. No structures were impacted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall, primarily over the high terrain just east of U.S. Highway 191/U.S. Highway 6 near milepost 292. Radar estimates showed roughly 1 to 2 inches of rain over the course of around an hour to an hour and a half. This resulted in a flow of water, mud and debris, including railroad ties from an adjacent railroad, to flow over the highway in the vicinity of milepost 292. It was later reported that this led to a family's SUV being washed off of the road, and the family required rescue. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong microburst near Erda, UT was observed by a live area webcam. Multiple pictures were later received showing a large tree downed, roof shingles blown off, play structures damaged and/or blown over, fences damaged, a house under construction damaged, and a center pivot damaged. Based on the damage pictures, an estimated wind gust of around 70 mph was determined.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall over the high terrain near the town of Hildale over the course of an hour, with Multi-Radar/Multi-Sensor 1-hour estimates of around 1 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Light to moderate rain persisted for 2 to 3 hours thereafter. The rainfall resulted in a strong flow in Short Creek through Hildale and on into Colorado City. Dispatch from Colorado City reported all street crossings across the creek were closed due to flooding, and 3 feet of fast moving water was reported to be flowing across Hildale Street.

Multi-Radar/Multi-Sensor rainfall 1-hour estimates were around 1 to 1.5 inches in the vicinity of Birch Creek, near the confluence with the Escalante River. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another active setup in mid-late July resulted in numerous microburst related wind gusts in portions of Utah's West Desert Region, as well as a report of severe hail. Additionally, flash flooding was noted in southern Utah. This also coincided with a period of anomalous heat across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of moderate, to at times strong, thunderstorms moved through Capitol Reef National Park over the span of 2 to 3 hours. Multi-Radar/Multi-Sensor 3-hour rainfall estimates showed around 1 to 2 inches within the park. As a result, a flow of 6-8 inches of water moved through Capitol Gorge, as reported by Park officials.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture resulted in reports of flash flooding in portions of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall in the high terrain around the town of Hildale, as well as within the town itself. Multi-Radar/Multi-Sensor (MRMS) 1-hour estimates showed around 1 to 2 inches of rainfall with this storm. Light to moderate rain continued for a while thereafter, with 3-hour MRMS estimates exceeding 2 inches of rainfall in spots. This rainfall ran into Short Creek, and resulted in a flow through the town of Hildale, though the flow was not significant enough to overtop any road crossings within town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture resulted in reports of flash flooding in portions of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall over the course of an hour or so in the high terrain northwest of the town of Leeds, UT. Multi-Radar/Multi-Sensor 1-hour estimates show around 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rainfall with this storm. An initial report was received of water over the bridge at Red Cliffs Campground as water flowed out of Water Canyon and through Harrisburg Creek. A short time later, a video was found on Twitter showing water flowing through the campground and overtopping the access bridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Utah Department of Transportation I-80 at MP 107 mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 71 mph. The wind gust was associated with microburst outflow winds from a nearby thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow moving Pacific trough and associated surface cold front finally progressed into the Great Basin late in the night and into the early morning hours. This led to moderate snow across the mountains of eastern Utah. Strong winds accompanied the front with gusts exceeding 45 mph throughout much of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 60 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously cold upper level trough dropped south from the Northern Rockies into eastern Utah. A strong cold front associated with the trough pushed across the area resulting in gusty winds to 45 mph in the mountains. Additionally, the front brought moderate snow to the La Sal Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 7 inches of snow fell in the La Sal Mountains, much of that in 12 hours. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unsettled weather pattern persisted during much of the month of November. This resulted in above normal precipitation for portions of eastern Utah. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas. Elsewhere, conditions remained relatively unchanged through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across the western portion of the zone through the month of November as extreme (D3) conditions became severe (D2). The severe drought over the central portions of the zone improved to the moderate (D1) category while moderate to abnormally dry (D0) conditions persisted over the far eastern area through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved slightly through the month of November. The extreme (D3) drought over the far western tier of the zone was trimmed back slightly at the northwest border as it improved to the severe (D2) category. Elsewhere, severe conditions persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low coming ashore along the U.S. West Coast resulted in a combination of strong forcing, instability, and available moisture over the Salt Lake City forecast area. This event prompted an uncommon issuance of a Severe Thunderstorm Watch (number 541 of the year). Scattered strong wind gusts occurred, as did multiple instances of Flash Flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Camel Back MTN weather station recorded an initial wind gust of 59 mph at 2:20 pm MST in association with nearby thunderstorm outflow winds. After a brief lull with lighter winds, an additional nearby shower resulted in more strong outflow winds which yielded a peak wind gust of 64 mph at 4:40 pm MST, and a final strong wind gust of 60 mph at 4:45 pm MST.

As a result, the North Fork Virgin at Springdale river gauge recorded a rapid rise in stage from around 50 CFS to over 1200 CFS in under 2 hours. Additionally, park rangers at Zion National Park reported flooding in many slot canyons in the park, as well as several observed waterfalls.

Multi-Radar/Multi-Sensor 1-hour rainfall estimates were in the 0.50 to 0.75 inch range. This was enough to result in a flow along Soldier Creek, which resulted in the evacuation of one house by State Route 36 and a road closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low coming ashore along the U.S. West Coast resulted in a combination of strong forcing, instability, and available moisture over the Salt Lake City forecast area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms with moderate to heavy rainfall moved over Bryce Canyon National Park during the late evening hours. Multi-Radar/Multi-Sensor 3-hour rainfall estimates showed widespread amounts of 1 to 2 inches, and the Bryce Canyon Airport ASOS recorded nearly 1.5 inches of rain over the span of 3:30 pm MST to 4:30 pm MST. Many trails sustained damage, with some leading to extended trail closures. The Queen's Garden trail suffered a few debris flows with unstable footing and two retaining wall failures. The Peekaboo Loop suffered numerous debris flows and points of trail surface erosion. The Bryce Point Peekaboo Connecting Trail suffered numerous rockfalls and debris flows, two retaining wall failures, and deep incisions of around 1 foot into the trail surface. The Fairyland Loop trail suffered deep incisions of around 1 foot into the trail surface. The Navajo Loop trail had a tree downed on the Two Bridges side. The Wall Street side of the Navajo Loop Trail suffered further debris flows and complete trail erosion in some sections, resulting in an extended trail closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture increased across the region as the area of high pressure shifted towards the Southern Plains. The abundance of moisture fueled numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. A debris flow occurred on the Pack Creek Fire burn scar as a result of a large amount of heavy rain in a short period of time which then flowed downstream to impact other areas such as near Moab which experienced some flash flooding. Additionally, thunderstorms produced damaging winds outside of Moab.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several side streets and low lying areas flooded along Mill and Pack Creeks as a result of heavy rain over the Pack Creek Fire burn scar. Water ran out of its banks in many spots. The Mill Creek stream gauge in Moab increased by 9 feet in 2 hours. Additionally, areas near Moab reported 0.80 to 1.2 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel Pacific low pressure system undercut the area of high pressure over the Great Basin as it dropped into the Northern Rockies. Monsoon moisture increased across the region with precipitable water values ranging from 125 to 175% of normal across the Four Corners. Clearing conditions the morning of the 20th led to increased instability over southeast Utah which led to some stronger thunderstorms during the late evening hours. The anomalously high moisture values across the area resulted in heavy rain with thunderstorms. Additionally, winds aloft supported the training of thunderstorms. This heavy rainfall led to widespread flash flooding and debris flows across portions of Grand and San Juan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms produced upwards of 1 to 2 inches of rain within one to two hours in an around Moab. The Mill Creek Parkway - a recreational path that runs through the center of Moab, was reported to be ���completely decimated��� as a result of the flooding. Several areas had their water turned off after flood waters led to a broken water main.  Numerous homes and businesses had upwards of 12 inches of water and debris inside resulting in significant property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of moisture associated with an Atmospheric River surged across eastern Utah with precipitable water values increasing up to 400% of normal. A Pacific system moved across the region which, when combined with the well above normal moisture, resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall across the mountains of eastern Utah. Light to moderate snow also fell in the Eastern Uinta Basin. Winds gusted to almost 50 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 7 inches of snow fell at the East Willow Creek SNOTEL site. Winds gusted to almost 50 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive arctic air mass accompanied a strong cold front that dropped across eastern Utah. The lift from the front itself paired with strong jet support aloft and minor moisture resulted in scattered showers across the Eastern Uinta Mountains. While snowfall totals weren't overly impressive with this system, generally light to moderate, the combination of snowfall with wind gusts in excess of 70 mph at times in the higher terrain resulted in blizzard-like conditions and significant travel impacts.||In the wake of the system, the arctic air overhead paired with continued breezy conditions resulted in bitterly cold wind chills for the northern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 50 and 80 mph at times which resulted in significantly reduced visibility in blowing snow and hazardous travel. There was a peak wind gust of over 100 mph recorded at the automated station at Chepeta on the Utah/Colorado state line. Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area. The cold front brought wind chills down to -30 degrees F at the automated station at Bassett Spring.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed near the summit of US-191 and Argyle Canyon |Road in remote southwestern Duchesne County at about 9200 feet. |Sporadic damage is noted in this area as it moved north over a |ridge line and into a valley adjacent to US-191 along Indian |Canyon.||The tornado then descended down the length of the valley from |about 9200 feet to a creek adjacent to US-191 at an elevation of |about 8500 feet. Over this period, both the strength and and width|of the tornado increased substantially with a continuous damage |path through a mixed aspen and conifer forest. The maximum damage |was found in this area and was significant. Almost all trees over |a large hillside facing the road were violently blown down, |snapped, or topped into a clearly convergent axis. Several tree |tops were desposited along the creek and road with no clear |indicatation as to where they orginated.||The tornado then crossed US-191 and ascended the hillside from |about 8500 feet to another ridge on the northwest side of the road|to an elevation of about 9100 feet. Considerable damage was noted|immediately north of US-191 where numerous trees were snapped or |uprooted, although not to the severe extent noted on the south |side of the road. Damage decreased considerably up the hillside to|the top of the 9100 foot ridge line with more sporadic toppled |trees.||A few toppled trees were noted into the next basin over the ridge|on the north side of the road, marking the last discernible |damage from the tornado, and the end of the 2 mile damage path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture associated with a landfalling moderate Atmospheric River reached eastern Utah, riding on a 120-130 knot jet aloft. Light to moderate snow also fell in some of the valley areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unsettled weather pattern persisted during much of the month of December. This resulted in above normal precipitation for portions of eastern Utah. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas. Elsewhere, conditions remained relatively unchanged through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained relatively unchanged through the month of December. Extreme (D3) drought persisted over the far western corner of the zone with severe (D2) conditions elsewhere through the end of the month. The severe drought did improve to the moderate (D1) category on the eastern border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure dominated the Four Corners region. This resulted in scorching heat and dry weather across southeast Utah. Conditions were exceptionally dry which translated to minimum humidities in the single digits to low teens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures of 103 to 110 degrees were reported by various automated weather stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure dominated the Four Corners region. This resulted in scorching heat and dry weather across southeast Utah. Conditions were exceptionally dry which translated to minimum humidities in the single digits to low teens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures of 98 to 104 degrees were reported by various automated weather stations. On the evening of Sunday, July 17, Canyonlands National Park staff received a report of a missing male subject who was overdue after attempting a short hike from Elephant Hill in the Needles District that afternoon. The body of the missing hiker was later found on Tuesday, July 19. The hiker is suspected to have died from heat-related illness.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unsettled weather pattern persisted during much of the month of September. This resulted in above normal precipitation for much of eastern Utah. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of September. Extreme (D3) drought persisted over the far western corner of the zone with severe (D2) conditions elsewhere through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unsettled weather pattern persisted during much of the month of September. This resulted in above normal precipitation for much of eastern Utah. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of September. Extreme (D3) conditions persisted across the western portion of the zone with severe (D2) conditions over the central zones. Meanwhile, moderate (D1) conditions prevailed across the far eastern portion of the zone through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow over several days brought accumulations of 12 or more to the northern Utah mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 22.0 inches with 1.28 inches of snow water equivalent was observed at Brighton Crest. A snowfall accumulation of 15.0 inches with 0.78 inches of snow water equivalent was observed at Silver Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the state on August 1, with several producing damaging outflow winds across northern Utah and isolated flash flooding in central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large debris flow originating off of the Dollar Ridge Fire burn scar passed through the Strawberry Pinnacles area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding over several days, which included Zion National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flows increased on the North Fork of the Virgin River in Zion National Park. The trail became impassable near the mouth of The Narrows. Flood waters in The Narrows swept some hikers off their feet and isolated others on higher ground. Two people were swept downstream. One was swept a couple of hundred yards and found. The individual survived with minor injuries. The other was reported as missing to Zion National Park officials later that evening, and was found deceased the following Monday. The North Fork Virgin - Springdale river gauge, which is downstream from The Narrows, rose from approximately 75 cfs to 735 cfs, corresponding to a rise of slightly over 2 feet in height.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought gusty winds and flash flooding to portions of Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Cannonville and Henrieville, with mud and debris on state Route 12 between the towns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another November system brought high winds to many locations throughout Utah. The system also brought snow and rain to many locations, with some appreciable accumulations noted across many Utah mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Kolob, Brian Head, and Clayton Springs recorded between around 1.2 to 3.7 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 14 to 44 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another November system brought high winds to many locations throughout Utah. The system also brought snow and rain to many locations, with some appreciable accumulations noted across many Utah mountains.

The Farmington SNOTEL station recorded the highest in the zone, with 3.55 inches of snow water equivalent, or approximately 41 inches of snow. In addition, many automated weather stations recorded strong wind gusts, including 82 mph at Ogden Peak, 81 mph at LOGAN PEAK, and 80 mph at Peter Sinks Rim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A head-on crash on snow covered roads killed one driver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports indicated a fatal head-on crash in snow occurred around 10:50 a.m. Road conditions from the nearest road weather sensor (US-40 @ Daniels Summit) indicated wind gusts peaked at 28 mph at 10 a.m. Road grip was decreased from 9:45 a.m. through the remainder of the morning, and was at it's lowest at 10:25 a.m. Visibility dropped below 1 mile around 9:45 a.m., and remained below 1 mile the remainder of the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure progressed across the Northern Rockies and lifted across eastern Utah. Ample moisture and favorable orographics led to moderate snow across the mountains. Moderate accumulations also occurred in the Eastern Uinta Basin. Winds gusted in excess of 45 mph in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 40 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture remained trapped across the region in the wake of a departing system. This led to the development of dense fog across the Eastern Uinta Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Eastern Uinta Basin including at the Vernal Regional Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours overnight and into the next morning.
